further_reading.title: Genesis 1-15 (Word Biblical Commentary)

images.caption: Wrestling with hard texts — the darkness at the crucifixion

title: How Did Judas Die?
question: Matthew says Judas hanged himself; Acts says he fell and burst open. Which is true?

responses.summary: MacArthur argues that the two accounts describe different stages of the same event. Judas hanged himself (Matthew), and at some point afterward — whether because the rope broke, the branch gave way, or the body was cut down — the corpse fell and burst open (Acts). In the climate of the Judean hill country, a body left hanging would quickly bloat and become distended; a fall from height onto rocks would produce exactly the result Acts describes. The field purchase is attributed to Judas in Acts because the money was technically his — the priests bought the field with Judas's returned blood money, and Luke attributes the purchase to Judas in the sense that it was done with his funds and on his account. This type of compressed attribution is common in ancient narrative. MacArthur notes that neither account claims to give a complete picture; each selects details relevant to its theological purpose.
responses.strengths: Provides a plausible physical sequence that accounts for both descriptions. The indirect attribution of the field purchase to Judas is linguistically defensible. Neither author claims comprehensiveness.
responses.weaknesses: The harmonization requires adding details present in neither text. The sequence of hanging → falling → bursting is possible but not stated. The different attributions of the field purchase remain awkward even with the explanation.

responses.summary: Keener emphasizes that ancient historians routinely selected different details from the same event to serve their narrative purposes. Matthew focuses on Judas's remorse and the fulfillment of Jeremiah/Zechariah's prophecy about the thirty silver coins — his interest is in the money and what happened to it. Luke in Acts focuses on Judas's gruesome end as divine judgment — his interest is in the fate of the betrayer and the need to replace him among the Twelve. These are not competing accounts but different angles on a complex event, comparable to how modern news reports of the same incident can emphasize different details and appear contradictory when read side by side. Keener notes that the verb in Acts 1:18 (prēnēs genomenos, 'falling headlong') could describe the aftermath of a hanging rather than an independent cause of death. The apparent contradictions arise from modern expectations of comprehensive, synchronized reporting that ancient writers did not share.
responses.strengths: Grounded in documented ancient historiographic practice. The different narrative purposes are clearly identifiable. Avoids forcing either text to say more than it claims.
responses.weaknesses: The 'different perspectives' explanation can become a universal harmonization tool that prevents any genuine contradiction from being recognized. The differences in this case are more substantial than typical complementary reporting.

responses.summary: Marcus argues that Matthew and Acts preserve independent traditions about Judas's death that cannot be harmonized without importing details from outside both texts. The differences are not merely perspectival but substantive: in Matthew, the priests buy the field; in Acts, Judas buys it. In Matthew, Judas hangs himself; in Acts, he falls and bursts. In Matthew, the field is named because it was bought with blood money; in Acts, because of the blood spilled on it. These are different etiologies for 'the Field of Blood,' suggesting two separate traditions developed to explain the same place name. Marcus notes that the death of a traitor was a common topos in ancient literature (cf. the deaths of Ahithophel, Antiochus IV, Nadan in the Story of Ahiqar), and different communities could develop distinct narrative traditions about such a death. Both versions serve the same theological purpose: the betrayer came to a terrible end, and even his death fulfilled Scripture.
responses.strengths: Takes the differences at face value without forcing harmonization. The comparison to Ahithophel's death (also hanging, 2 Samuel 17:23) suggests Matthew draws on biblical typology. The two different etiologies for 'Field of Blood' are genuinely distinct.
responses.weaknesses: The 'independent traditions' conclusion does not prove the accounts are irreconcilable — they could still describe different aspects of the same event. The theological agreement (divine judgment on the betrayer) may indicate a common historical core beneath the variant traditions.

responses.summary: Calvin addressed this discrepancy in his commentary on Acts, acknowledging the apparent contradiction while arguing that both accounts are truthful reports shaped by different rhetorical purposes. Calvin noted that Matthew's account draws on the typology of Ahithophel — David's trusted counselor who betrayed him and hanged himself (2 Samuel 17:23) — presenting Judas as the antitype of the archetypical traitor. Luke's account in Acts emphasizes the physical horror of divine judgment, employing language reminiscent of the deaths of the wicked in the OT (cf. the death of the Judean king Jehoram in 2 Chronicles 21:18–19). Calvin argued that both writers selected details that connected Judas's death to established scriptural patterns of judgment, and that the apparent contradictions arise because each writer prioritized typological resonance over comprehensive physical description. The theological truth — that God judged the betrayer — is the point both texts are making.
responses.strengths: Identifies genuine scriptural typologies behind both accounts. Takes both texts seriously as theological narration. The Ahithophel parallel in Matthew is widely recognized.
responses.weaknesses: Typological analysis explains why the writers chose their details but does not resolve whether the details are historically compatible. The approach may seem to prioritize theological meaning over historical accuracy.

context: Matthew 27:3–5 reports that Judas, seized with remorse after betraying Jesus, returned the thirty silver coins to the chief priests and then 'went away and hanged himself.' Acts 1:18–19 gives a strikingly different account: 'With the payment he received for his wickedness, Judas bought a field; there he fell headlong, his body burst open and all his intestines spilled out.' The accounts differ on who purchased the field (the priests in Matthew, Judas in Acts), how Judas died (hanging vs. falling), and the circumstances of his death. This is one of the most frequently cited contradictions in the NT.

key_verses.text: So Judas threw the money into the temple and left. Then he went away and hanged himself.
With the payment he received for his wickedness, Judas bought a field; there he fell headlong, his body burst open and all his intestines spilled out.
So they decided to use the money to buy the potter’s field as a burial place for foreigners.

consensus: Evangelical scholars generally harmonize the accounts as complementary (e.g., Judas hanged himself and the body later fell and burst). Critical scholars typically view the accounts as independent, irreconcilable traditions. Most scholars on both sides agree that the theological point — Judas's terrible end as judgment for betrayal — is the primary concern of both writers.

images.caption: Wrestling with hard texts — the darkness at the crucifixion

title: Who Incited David's Census?
question: Did God or Satan incite David to number Israel?

responses.tradition: Dual Causation / Primary and Secondary Agency
responses.summary: Selman argues that both texts are correct because they describe different levels of causation operating simultaneously. In the biblical worldview, God's sovereign will and creaturely agency are not mutually exclusive: God can accomplish his purposes through secondary agents, including adversarial ones. The parallel with Job is exact: God permits Satan to act, and the resulting events are attributed to both God (who permitted them) and Satan (who executed them). In 2 Samuel, the author focuses on God's sovereign permission — the census happened because God's anger against Israel required a vehicle for judgment. In Chronicles, the author focuses on the immediate agent — Satan was the one who directly incited David. Selman notes that this is not a contradiction but a complementary perspective on a single event viewed from two legitimate theological angles. Reformed theology has formalized this as the distinction between God's decretive will and his permissive will.
responses.strengths: The Job parallel provides an exact canonical model for dual causation. Consistent with the biblical pattern of divine sovereignty working through secondary agents. Preserves the truthfulness of both accounts.
responses.weaknesses: The 2 Samuel text says God 'incited' David directly, not merely 'permitted' Satan — the language is stronger than the Job parallel suggests. The dual-causation framework may impose a systematic theology on texts that are making different theological points.

responses.summary: Japhet identifies this as a clear case of theological reinterpretation by the Chronicler. In earlier Israelite theology, all events — including calamities and evil impulses — were attributed directly to God as the sole ultimate cause (cf. Isaiah 45:7, 'I form the light and create darkness, I bring prosperity and create disaster'). By the post-exilic period when Chronicles was written, Israel's theology had developed a more differentiated understanding of evil's agency, influenced partly by Persian dualism and partly by internal theological reflection. The Chronicler was uncomfortable attributing the incitement to evil directly to God and substituted 'Satan' (with or without the definite article — the Hebrew is ambiguous) as the proximate cause. Japhet argues this is not a 'correction' of Samuel but a legitimate theological restatement: the Chronicler preserves the historical event while updating the theological framework through which it is understood.
responses.strengths: Accounts for the change as intentional theological reinterpretation, not scribal error. Well-grounded in the documented development of Israelite angelology and demonology. Takes the Chronicler seriously as a theologian, not just a copyist.
responses.weaknesses: The 'development' language can imply that earlier theology was wrong or primitive. The Persian influence on Israelite Satan-theology is debated. Evangelical readers may resist the implication that Scripture's theology 'evolved.'

responses.summary: MacArthur resolves the difficulty by appealing to God's sovereign governance of all events, including those accomplished through adversarial agents. When 2 Samuel says God incited David, it reflects the comprehensive biblical principle that nothing happens outside God's sovereign will — even Satan operates only within divinely established boundaries (Job 1–2). When Chronicles says Satan incited David, it identifies the immediate spiritual agent who planted the desire in David's heart. MacArthur draws the analogy to 1 Kings 22:19–23, where God sends a 'deceiving spirit' to accomplish his purposes through Ahab's false prophets — an event simultaneously attributed to God's decree and the spirit's agency. The census was sinful because David acted out of pride and self-reliance rather than trust in God, but God used David's sin to bring about the revelation of the temple site (2 Samuel 24:18–25) — turning judgment into redemptive provision.
responses.strengths: Consistent with the broader biblical pattern of divine sovereignty over evil. The 1 Kings 22 parallel is directly relevant. The redemptive outcome (temple site) provides a theological framework for God's involvement.
responses.weaknesses: The distinction between God 'causing' and 'permitting' evil is theologically important but may not resolve the tension in the text itself. 2 Samuel's language ('he incited') is active, not permissive.

responses.summary: Block reads the Chronicler not as contradicting Samuel but as offering an authoritative theological interpretation of the earlier text. The Chronicler, writing for a post-exilic audience, had access to Samuel-Kings and deliberately chose to present the same event through a different theological lens. By attributing the incitement to 'Satan' (or 'an adversary' — the Hebrew satan can mean either the supernatural figure or simply 'an opponent'), the Chronicler shifts the emphasis from God's inscrutable sovereignty to the reality of spiritual opposition that God's people face. Block notes that this interpretive practice is not unique: the NT similarly reinterprets OT events through new theological categories (Hebrews 11 reframes numerous OT narratives). The Chronicler's restatement does not contradict Samuel any more than Hebrews contradicts Genesis — both are authoritative interpretations of the same events from different vantage points within the canonical tradition.
responses.strengths: Takes the Chronicler's theological agency seriously. The analogy to NT reinterpretation of OT events is insightful. Preserves the authority of both texts without forcing mechanical harmonization.
responses.weaknesses: The comparison to Hebrews is imperfect — Hebrews adds theological interpretation, while Chronicles appears to change the agent of the action. The 'interpretive theologian' label may not satisfy those who expect historical narratives to report the same facts consistently.

context: 2 Samuel 24:1 states: 'Again the anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and he incited David against them, saying, "Go and take a census of Israel and Judah."' But 1 Chronicles 21:1 records the same event differently: 'Satan rose up against Israel and incited David to take a census of Israel.' The same event is attributed to two different agents — God and Satan. This is perhaps the clearest example of a theological development between the earlier and later historical books, and it raises fundamental questions about divine sovereignty, secondary causation, and how Israel's understanding of evil developed over time.

key_verses.text: Again the anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and he incited David against them, saying, “Go and take a census of Israel and Judah.”
Satan rose up against Israel and incited David to take a census of Israel.
The LORD said to Satan, “Very well, then, everything he has is in your power, but on the man himself do not lay a finger.”

consensus: Most scholars — evangelical and critical — recognize that the Chronicler's substitution of 'Satan' for 'the LORD' reflects a theological development in Israel's understanding of evil's agency. The dominant evangelical reading sees this as complementary (God permitted what Satan instigated), while critical scholars emphasize the theological evolution between the two texts.

images.caption: Wrestling with hard texts — the darkness at the crucifixion

title: Matthew and Luke's Genealogies
question: Why are Jesus's genealogies in Matthew and Luke so different?

responses.summary: MacArthur presents the most widely held evangelical solution: Matthew gives Joseph's legal genealogy (the royal line through Solomon, establishing Jesus' legal right to the throne of David), while Luke gives Mary's biological lineage (through Nathan, establishing Jesus' physical descent from David). Under this view, 'Heli' in Luke 3:23 is Mary's father, and Joseph is called 'son of Heli' because he became Heli's legal son through marriage — a recognized convention in Jewish practice where a man without sons could adopt his son-in-law into his genealogical line. Luke's parenthetical 'so it was thought' (Luke 3:23) signals that he is aware of the virgin birth and is tracing the biological line through Mary rather than the legal line through Joseph. This explains why the two genealogies diverge after David: they follow different branches of David's descendants, converging again at Joseph and Mary.
responses.strengths: Explains the divergence cleanly — two different lines of descent from David. Consistent with the theological purposes of each Gospel (Matthew: royal legitimacy; Luke: universal humanity through Adam). The 'so it was thought' parenthetical in Luke may signal awareness of the distinction.
responses.weaknesses: Luke explicitly names Joseph, not Mary, as the genealogical subject. No ancient source identifies the Luke genealogy as Mary's. The son-in-law convention, while plausible, is not clearly attested for genealogical usage in this period.

responses.summary: Keener presents the levirate marriage theory, which has roots in Julius Africanus (3rd century AD). Under Jewish levirate law (Deuteronomy 25:5–6), if a man died without sons, his brother was obligated to marry the widow and produce an heir who would legally be counted as the deceased man's son. If Jacob (Matthew's name for Joseph's father) and Heli (Luke's name) were half-brothers who shared a mother but had different fathers, and if one died childless, the other would have married his widow. Joseph would then have been the biological son of one man and the legal heir of the other — explaining why both genealogies legitimately name different fathers. Julius Africanus claimed this tradition came from Jesus' own relatives (the desposynoi), giving it early provenance. Keener notes that the theory is complex but has the virtue of taking both genealogies at face value as lines through Joseph.
responses.strengths: Takes both genealogies at face value as Joseph's lineage. Has ancient attestation from Julius Africanus (c. 220 AD). Levirate marriage was a genuine institution in Jewish practice.
responses.weaknesses: The theory requires a specific and unattested family situation (half-brothers with a shared mother). Julius Africanus's source (the desposynoi) cannot be independently verified. The complexity of the solution may suggest it is more ingenious than historical.

responses.summary: Longman argues that both genealogies are primarily theological documents, not biological records, and should be interpreted in light of their respective Gospels' purposes. Matthew structures his genealogy into three sets of fourteen generations (Abraham→David, David→Exile, Exile→Christ) — a numerological scheme that requires selective inclusion and omission of known kings (Ahaziah, Joash, and Amaziah are skipped between Jehoram and Uzziah). Luke extends his genealogy back to 'Adam, the son of God,' emphasizing Jesus' connection to all humanity. Both evangelists are making theological arguments about who Jesus is: Matthew presents him as the climax of Israel's royal-covenantal history; Luke presents him as the new Adam, the representative of the human race. Longman suggests that attempting to harmonize every name in the two lists may miss the point — these are curated theological statements, not census records.
responses.strengths: Correctly identifies the theological structuring in both genealogies. Matthew's omission of known kings proves he is curating, not transcribing. The different endpoints (Abraham vs. Adam) reveal different theological purposes.
responses.weaknesses: Theological purpose does not exclude historical accuracy — the genealogies could be both structured and factual. This view may not explain why the names diverge so sharply after David. Can be perceived as avoiding the harmonization question.

responses.summary: Waltke proposes a variation of the traditional view: Matthew traces the royal (legal) line of succession to David's throne, while Luke traces the natural (biological) line. These need not be identical — royal succession does not always follow strict primogeniture, and adoptions, lateral successions, and dynastic disruptions (like the exile) could produce two legitimate but different lines. Matthew's interest in the kingship theme (he begins 'the son of David, the son of Abraham' and structures his genealogy around David and the exile) explains why he follows the royal line through Solomon and the kings of Judah. Luke, writing for a Gentile audience and emphasizing Jesus' full humanity, follows the biological line that may have passed through non-royal branches of David's family. Waltke notes that both lines converge on the critical theological point: Jesus is a descendant of David, and thus qualified to be the Messiah.
responses.strengths: Explains the divergence through a recognized distinction between royal succession and biological descent. Both lines establish Davidic lineage, which is the essential theological claim. Consistent with the known complexity of ancient Near Eastern royal genealogies.
responses.weaknesses: The theory is somewhat speculative — neither Gospel explicitly identifies its line as 'royal' vs. 'natural.' The sharp divergence after David still requires explanation for each individual name. The distinction may not account for all the differences.

context: Matthew 1:1–17 traces Jesus' ancestry from Abraham through David and the royal line to Joseph. Luke 3:23–38 traces it from Jesus back through David to Adam. The genealogies diverge sharply after David: Matthew follows the royal line through Solomon, while Luke follows a different line through Nathan (another son of David). The names from David to Joseph are almost entirely different in the two lists. Both claim to give Joseph's lineage, yet they name different fathers for Joseph — Matthew says Jacob, Luke says Heli. The number of generations also differs: Matthew lists 28 generations from David to Jesus, Luke lists 43.

key_verses.text: and Jacob the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary, and Mary was the mother of Jesus who is called the Messiah.
Now Jesus himself was about thirty years old when he began his ministry. He was the son, so it was thought, of Joseph, the son of Heli.
This is the genealogy of Jesus the Messiah the son of David, the son of Abraham.

consensus: No single solution has achieved consensus. The 'Mary's line in Luke' theory is the most popular evangelical explanation but faces the difficulty that Luke explicitly names Joseph. The levirate marriage theory and the theological-purpose view each have scholarly support. Most scholars agree that the genealogies serve primarily theological purposes — establishing Jesus' Davidic credentials and messianic legitimacy.

further_reading.title: The Birth of the Messiah

further_reading.title: Matthew (Smyth & Helwys Commentary)

images.caption: Wrestling with hard texts — the darkness at the crucifixion

title: Resurrection Morning Differences
question: Why do the four Gospels tell the resurrection story so differently?

responses.summary: Keener argues that the differences among the resurrection accounts are precisely what one would expect from independent eyewitness testimony about a confusing, emotionally overwhelming event. Modern forensic research consistently shows that multiple witnesses to the same event will report different details, emphasize different moments, and even disagree on specifics like the number of people present or the exact sequence of events — while agreeing on the core facts. The resurrection accounts exhibit exactly this pattern: the core narrative (empty tomb, angelic announcement, risen Jesus) is consistent, while peripheral details (how many angels, which women, exact timing) vary. Keener notes that ancient biographers understood this principle: Thucydides, Polybius, and other historians expected accounts to differ in detail while agreeing in substance. The variations actually strengthen the case for historicity, because fabricated accounts would be harmonized.
responses.strengths: Well-grounded in modern research on eyewitness testimony. The 'fabricated stories would agree' argument is logically sound. The core agreement amid peripheral variation matches genuine testimony patterns.
responses.weaknesses: Some differences go beyond peripheral detail — the sequence of appearances and the women's responses differ substantially. The eyewitness analogy assumes the accounts derive from direct testimony, which not all scholars accept. At what point do 'variations' become 'contradictions'?

responses.summary: MacArthur argues that each Gospel writer selected details from the full resurrection narrative that served his particular theological purpose and audience. Matthew, writing for a Jewish audience, emphasizes the earthquake, the angel's dramatic descent, and the guards' report — elements that address the Jewish objection that the disciples stole the body. Mark, characteristically terse, focuses on the women's fear and the command to tell the disciples — consistent with his messianic secret theme. Luke emphasizes the two men's reminder of Jesus' Galilean predictions and the road to Emmaus — connecting the resurrection to Jesus' teaching ministry. John focuses on Mary Magdalene's personal encounter with the risen Lord — consistent with his Gospel's emphasis on individual faith. No writer claims to give a complete account; each contributes a facet of a complex event that no single narrative could fully capture.
responses.strengths: Each Gospel's selection is demonstrably consistent with its broader themes and audience. The 'no writer claims completeness' principle is textually warranted. Explains apparent omissions without positing contradiction.
responses.weaknesses: Selectivity explains omissions but does not fully address cases where the accounts seem to conflict (e.g., the number of angels). The theological-purpose argument can become a catch-all that prevents any detail from being assessed historically.

responses.summary: Marcus views the resurrection accounts as preserving multiple independent traditions about the events of Easter morning that were transmitted and shaped within different early Christian communities before being incorporated into the written Gospels. The variations — different women named, different numbers of angels, different sequences of appearances — reflect the oral transmission process, in which different communities remembered and emphasized different aspects of the tradition. Marcus notes that the independence of the traditions is actually evidence for the historicity of the empty tomb claim: multiple independent attestation is a standard criterion of historical reliability, and the fact that traditions that developed separately all converge on the empty tomb and the angelic announcement strongly suggests a historical core. The variations are the expected result of oral tradition, not evidence of fabrication.
responses.strengths: Multiple independent attestation is a recognized historical criterion. The 1 Corinthians 15 tradition is very early (within 5 years of the crucifixion). Takes oral tradition seriously as a factor in textual variation.
responses.weaknesses: The 'independent traditions' model does not easily explain the literary relationships between the Synoptic accounts (Mark as a source for Matthew and Luke). The model may underestimate the degree of early Christian coordination and communication.

responses.summary: Longman reads the resurrection accounts as theological portraits — each evangelist painting the same event with different artistic emphases, much as four painters depicting the same landscape would produce recognizably different works. The number of angels is a classic example: mentioning one angel (Matthew, Mark) does not exclude the presence of two (Luke, John) — ancient writers frequently focused on the primary speaker or actor. The different women named at the tomb likely reflect each writer's relationship to different witnesses: John features Mary Magdalene prominently because she was his primary source for these events, while Mark names Mary Magdalene, Mary mother of James, and Salome. Longman argues that the theological framing is primary: Matthew's resurrection establishes Jesus as king, Mark's creates awe, Luke's fulfills prophecy, and John's restores relationship. The historical event is refracted through four theological lenses.
responses.strengths: The 'four painters' analogy communicates the principle effectively. The 'mentioning one does not exclude two' principle is logically sound. Each Gospel's theological emphasis is genuinely distinctive.
responses.weaknesses: The 'one does not exclude two' argument can feel like special pleading if applied too broadly. Not all differences reduce to emphasis — some involve sequence and content. The approach works better for some differences than others.

context: The four Gospels agree on the essential facts of the resurrection: women went to the tomb early Sunday morning, found it empty, and received angelic announcement that Jesus had risen. But the details diverge significantly. Matthew mentions one angel; Mark mentions a young man; Luke mentions two men; John mentions two angels. The women present differ across accounts. The sequence of appearances varies. The disciples' reactions are described differently. These differences have been cited both as evidence of fabrication (why can't the accounts agree?) and as evidence of authenticity (fabricated stories would be more consistent).

key_verses.text: The angel said to the women, “Do not be afraid, for I know that you are looking for Jesus, who was crucified. He is not here; he has risen, just as he said. Come and see the place where he lay.”
As they entered the tomb, they saw a young man dressed in a white robe sitting on the right side, and they were alarmed.

Early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark, Mary Magdalene went to the tomb and saw that the stone had been removed from the entrance. So she came running to Simon Peter and the other disciple.

consensus: Most scholars — whether they accept the historicity of the resurrection or not — agree that the diversity of the accounts argues against simple fabrication. If the early church invented the resurrection story, the accounts would likely be more harmonized. Evangelical scholars generally view the differences as complementary perspectives on a complex, chaotic morning. Critical scholars tend to see them as evidence of multiple independent traditions about a transformative experience.

further_reading.author: N.T. Wright
further_reading.title: The Resurrection of the Son of God

further_reading.author: Michael R. Licona
further_reading.title: The Resurrection of Jesus: A New Historiographical Approach

further_reading.author: Craig S. Keener
further_reading.title: The Historical Jesus of the Gospels

images.caption: Wrestling with hard texts — the darkness at the crucifixion

title: Sermon on the Mount vs. Plain
question: Did Jesus preach this sermon on a mountain (Matthew) or a level place (Luke)?

responses.summary: Keener argues that Matthew and Luke record the same event from different angles and with different editorial selection. The apparent location difference is easily resolved: a 'level place' on a mountainside is not a contradiction — a plateau or terrace on a hill would be both 'on a mountain' (Matthew's broader description of the setting) and 'a level place' (Luke's specific description of where Jesus stood). Ancient teachers regularly taught on hillsides where a natural amphitheater provided acoustics for large crowds. The differences in wording reflect each evangelist's editorial style and theological emphasis: Matthew's 'poor in spirit' makes explicit the spiritual dimension that Luke's 'poor' leaves implicit, while Luke's more concrete language reflects his consistent concern for the materially disadvantaged. Both versions faithfully represent Jesus' teaching; each writer has shaped the presentation for his audience.
responses.strengths: The 'level place on a mountainside' solution is geographically plausible and elegant. Both Gospels' editorial tendencies are well-documented. Itinerant teachers naturally repeat material — a single sermon could produce variant reports.
responses.weaknesses: Luke's version is much shorter (30 verses vs. Matthew's 107), suggesting significant editorial shaping by one or both writers. The differences in the Beatitudes go beyond style to substance ('poor' vs. 'poor in spirit'). The 'same event' theory must account for substantial content differences.

responses.summary: MacArthur argues that Jesus preached similar sermons on at least two occasions — once on a mountainside (Matthew) and once on a level place (Luke). As an itinerant teacher who traveled throughout Galilee, Judea, and beyond for approximately three years, Jesus would naturally have repeated his core teachings many times, adapting emphasis and illustration to different audiences and settings. Modern pastors preach the same essential message repeatedly with variations; there is no reason to assume Jesus taught each lesson only once. The overlapping content reflects a common body of teaching, while the differences reflect distinct occasions. MacArthur notes that the surrounding narrative contexts differ: Matthew places the sermon early in the Galilean ministry as a programmatic statement; Luke places it after the selection of the Twelve, as instruction for the newly formed apostolic band.
responses.strengths: Avoids forcing the accounts into artificial harmony. An itinerant teacher repeating core material is entirely natural. The different narrative contexts support distinct occasions.
responses.weaknesses: The high degree of verbal overlap suggests literary dependence rather than independent reports of separate events. If Jesus preached two nearly identical sermons, the 'two occasions' theory begins to resemble the 'one event' theory. Occam's razor favors one sermon over two.

responses.summary: Marcus and other scholars following the two-source hypothesis argue that Matthew's Sermon on the Mount is a composite discourse: Matthew has gathered Jesus' sayings from multiple occasions and sources (Q, M, and his own editorial work) into a single literary sermon, much as a modern editor might compile a speaker's 'greatest hits' into a single address. Luke's shorter Sermon on the Plain preserves a version closer to the Q source that both evangelists shared. This editorial practice was entirely conventional in ancient biography and historiography — Thucydides composed speeches for his historical figures based on 'what was appropriate to the occasion,' and ancient biographers regularly gathered their subjects' memorable sayings into set-piece discourses. Matthew's sermon is not a stenographic transcript but a carefully structured theological composition that faithfully represents the substance of Jesus' teaching even if it combines material from different settings.
responses.strengths: Explains the length difference (Matthew compiled more material). Consistent with ancient biographical composition practices. The Q hypothesis provides a concrete mechanism for the shared and divergent material.
responses.weaknesses: The Q hypothesis, while widely held, is not universally accepted. The composite discourse theory may imply that Matthew created a fictional narrative setting. Some find the conclusion that Jesus never actually preached this specific sermon troubling.

responses.summary: Longman draws attention to the theological significance of the settings in each Gospel. Matthew's 'mountainside' is a deliberate Moses typology: just as Moses received the law on Mount Sinai, Jesus — the new and greater Moses — delivers the new covenant teaching from a mountain. This Mosaic typology pervades Matthew's Gospel (five major discourse blocks echoing the five books of Moses, infancy narrative paralleling Moses' birth narrative). Luke's 'level place' reflects his theology of reversal and equality: Jesus stands among the people, accessible to the crowd, delivering blessings and woes that overturn social hierarchies. Longman argues that both evangelists may be describing the same event but framing the setting in language that serves their respective theological portraits of Jesus. The question 'Was it a mountain or a plain?' may impose a false choice on texts that are using geography symbolically as well as descriptively.
responses.strengths: The Mosaic typology in Matthew is well-documented and widely recognized. Luke's theology of reversal is a genuine literary pattern. Demonstrates how ancient writers could describe the same reality through different theological lenses.
responses.weaknesses: Theological symbolism does not exclude geographical specificity — the setting could be both literal and symbolic. If the geography is primarily symbolic, the question of historicity is sidestepped rather than answered. The argument works better if there was one event; if two, the theological geography is coincidental.

context: Matthew 5–7 records the Sermon on the Mount, introduced with 'he went up on a mountainside and sat down' (5:1). Luke 6:17–49 records a shorter parallel sermon, introduced with 'He went down with them and stood on a level place' (6:17). The sermons share much content (the Beatitudes, the Golden Rule, the parable of the wise builder) but differ in setting, length, and specific wording. Matthew's Beatitudes are spiritual ('poor in spirit,' 'hunger and thirst for righteousness'); Luke's are more physical ('you who are poor,' 'you who hunger now'). Were these the same sermon, different sermons, or composite teachings?

key_verses.text: Now when Jesus saw the crowds, he went up on a mountainside and sat down. His disciples came to him, and he began to teach them.

He went down with them and stood on a level place. A large crowd of his disciples was there and a great number of people from all Judea, from Jerusalem, and from the coastal region around Tyre and Sidon.
Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.

consensus: Evangelical scholars are divided between the 'two different sermons' and 'same sermon, different versions' views. Critical scholars generally regard them as different literary compositions based on a common tradition of Jesus' teachings. All sides agree that Jesus, as an itinerant teacher, would have repeated and adapted his core teachings in different settings.

images.caption: Wrestling with hard texts — the darkness at the crucifixion

title: God Hardening Pharaoh's Heart
question: If God hardened Pharaoh's heart, how can Pharaoh be held responsible?

responses.summary: Schreiner argues that God's hardening of Pharaoh is judicial — a divine response to Pharaoh's prior, voluntary rebellion. The narrative sequence matters: Pharaoh hardens his own heart through the first five plagues (Ex 7:13, 22; 8:15, 19, 32) before the text says God hardened him (Ex 9:12). God's hardening confirms and intensifies a direction Pharaoh freely chose. This pattern — God giving sinners over to the consequences of their own choices — recurs throughout Scripture (Romans 1:24, 26, 28). Paul's citation in Romans 9 does not introduce a new theology of arbitrary coercion but appeals to a case where God's sovereignty operated through, not against, human moral agency. Pharaoh was culpable because he was never an innocent victim; he was a ruthless tyrant whom God used to display redemptive power.
responses.strengths: Accounts for the narrative sequence where Pharaoh's self-hardening precedes divine hardening. Integrates the Exodus account with Paul's broader theology of divine 'giving over' in Romans 1. Preserves both sovereignty and responsibility.
responses.weaknesses: The pre-narrative announcement in Exodus 4:21 ('I will harden his heart') complicates the 'Pharaoh first' reading — God declares the hardening before it begins. Does not fully address whether God's prior decree removes genuine freedom.

responses.summary: Calvin insists that Scripture affirms both poles without offering philosophical resolution: God is sovereign over Pharaoh's will, and Pharaoh is fully culpable for his sin. Calvin rejects the idea that God merely permitted Pharaoh's stubbornness — the Hebrew verbs are active and causative, and Paul's argument in Romans 9 would lose its force if reduced to mere permission. Yet Calvin equally insists Pharaoh sinned voluntarily; no one is compelled to evil against their nature. The mystery is not a contradiction but a limitation of creaturely understanding. Calvin warns that demanding a philosophical reconciliation betrays the same presumption Paul rebukes in Romans 9:20 — 'Who are you, a human being, to talk back to God?' We are called to worship, not to resolve what God has left unresolved.
responses.strengths: Takes the strongest biblical language seriously without softening it. Historically grounded in the Reformed confessional tradition. Rightly identifies the hubris in demanding that divine action conform to human philosophical categories.
responses.weaknesses: Can appear to shut down legitimate moral inquiry by appealing to mystery. Critics argue that 'mystery' becomes a refuge from a genuine ethical problem rather than an honest theological posture.

responses.summary: Sarna argues that the Hebrew idiom attributes to God what God occasions or permits, not what God directly coerces. The plagues themselves were the 'hardening' mechanism — confronted with unmistakable divine power, Pharaoh chose defiance rather than submission. God 'hardened' Pharaoh's heart by creating the conditions (escalating plagues) that provoked Pharaoh's characteristic response. This reflects a broader Hebrew linguistic pattern in which causation is expressed in terms of the ultimate rather than the proximate agent. Sarna notes that the three different Hebrew verbs (ḥāzaq, kābēd, qāšâ) suggest strengthening, making heavy, and making obstinate — a spectrum that resists reduction to simple coercion. Pharaoh's response was genuinely his own, shaped by his character and pride.
responses.strengths: Rooted in careful attention to Hebrew idiom and Ancient Near Eastern literary conventions. Avoids imposing later theological categories on the Exodus narrative. Explains the alternating agency naturally.
responses.weaknesses: May not fully account for the pre-narrative declaration in Exodus 4:21 where God announces the hardening as his own action before any plagues occur. Paul's use in Romans 9 seems to press beyond mere occasionalism.

responses.summary: Brueggemann reads the hardening narrative as a theological commentary on empire. Pharaoh represents the imperial system that resists God's liberating purpose — a system so committed to its own power that it becomes incapable of recognizing or yielding to divine authority. The 'hardening' is not primarily about an individual's psychological state but about the way oppressive structures calcify against justice. God's hardening of Pharaoh functions narratively to demonstrate that no earthly power, however entrenched, can finally resist the God who liberates the enslaved. The text is less interested in Pharaoh's moral accountability than in Yahweh's sovereignty over all rival claims to power. The narrative invites readers to ask not 'Was Pharaoh free?' but 'What power can withstand the God of the Exodus?'
responses.strengths: Reads the text in its narrative context as a confrontation between Yahweh and Pharaoh-as-system. Captures the text's rhetorical force — the Exodus is about divine power over empire, not a philosophy seminar on free will. Resonates with the text's own emphasis on God's name and reputation.
responses.weaknesses: Can minimize the genuine philosophical and theological difficulty the text raises about individual moral agency. May read modern liberation theology back into an ancient text that does not share its conceptual framework.

responses.summary: Moo argues that Paul's use of the Pharaoh episode in Romans 9 should govern how Christians read Exodus. Paul presents God's hardening as an exercise of sovereign prerogative — 'God has mercy on whom he wants to have mercy, and he hardens whom he wants to harden' (9:18). Yet Paul immediately anticipates the objection ('Then why does God still blame us?', 9:19) and responds not by denying the tension but by asserting God's sovereign right as Creator. Moo contends that Paul holds a compatibilist position: divine determination and human responsibility coexist without one canceling the other. The 'hardening' operates within the broader argument of Romans 9-11, which culminates not in arbitrary judgment but in God's mercy to all (11:32). The Pharaoh case is not the whole story — it is one movement in a symphony that ends in mercy.
responses.strengths: Reads Exodus through Paul's own interpretive lens, which is theologically appropriate for a Christian study tool. Situates the hardening within the full Romans 9-11 arc rather than isolating it. Handles the tension honestly without dissolving either pole.
responses.weaknesses: Compatibilism is a philosophical framework not explicitly named in the text — critics argue it is being imported. The appeal to Romans 11:32 may not fully resolve the particular case of Pharaoh, whose destruction is the point of the illustration.

context: Exodus records a sequence where Pharaoh's heart is 'hardened' — sometimes by God (Ex 4:21; 7:3; 9:12; 10:1, 20, 27; 11:10; 14:4, 8), sometimes by Pharaoh himself (Ex 8:15, 32; 9:34). Three different Hebrew verbs are used: ḥāzaq (strengthen), kābēd (make heavy), and qāšâ (make obstinate). Paul cites the episode in Romans 9:17-18 to illustrate divine sovereignty. The difficulty: if God hardened Pharaoh's heart, how is Pharaoh morally culpable for refusing to let Israel go?

key_verses.text: The LORD said to Moses, 'When you return to Egypt, see that you perform before Pharaoh all the wonders I have given you the power to do. But I will harden his heart so that he will not let the people go.'
But when Pharaoh saw that there was relief, he hardened his heart and would not listen to Moses and Aaron, just as the LORD had said.
But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart and he would not listen to Moses and Aaron, just as the LORD had said to Moses.

For Scripture says to Pharaoh: 'I raised you up for this very purpose, that I might display my power in you and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.' Therefore God has mercy on whom he wants to have mercy, and he hardens whom he wants to harden.

consensus: Most scholars note the alternating agency — Pharaoh hardens himself first (Ex 7:13–8:32) before God confirms that hardening (Ex 9:12 onward). The text presents both divine sovereignty and human responsibility without philosophical resolution. The three Hebrew verbs suggest a complex process, not simple coercion.

images.caption: Wrestling with hard texts — the darkness at the crucifixion

title: The Unforgivable Sin
question: What is the unforgivable sin, and can a Christian commit it?

responses.summary: Schreiner argues that the blasphemy against the Spirit was the Pharisees' specific act of attributing Jesus's Spirit-empowered miracles to Satan — a deliberate, informed rejection of the Spirit's testimony to Christ. What makes this sin uniquely unforgivable is not its severity in isolation but its nature: it rejects the very agent (the Holy Spirit) through whom conviction, repentance, and faith come. If one permanently rejects the Spirit's witness, there is no alternative path to forgiveness. Schreiner stresses that this was not a momentary doubt or even a serious failure but a settled, willful attribution of divine work to demonic power by people who had sufficient evidence to know better. Christians troubled by this passage should note that their very anxiety demonstrates the Spirit is still active in their conscience — the unforgivable sin, by definition, involves no such concern.
responses.strengths: Grounds the sin in its specific narrative context rather than abstracting it into a general category. The logic — rejecting the Spirit removes the only means of repentance — is theologically coherent. Directly addresses pastoral anxiety.
responses.weaknesses: If the sin is entirely situation-specific (the earthly ministry of Jesus), it becomes irrelevant as a present-day warning, which seems to undercut the seriousness with which Jesus issues it.

responses.summary: Fee emphasizes the ongoing nature implied by the Greek present tense — this is not a single act but a persistent stance of attributing the Spirit's work to evil. The unforgivable sin is not a discrete moment but a hardened disposition: a settled, sustained, and willful rejection of the Holy Spirit's testimony about Christ. Fee, as a Pentecostal scholar deeply committed to the Spirit's ongoing activity, argues that blasphemy against the Spirit means fundamentally and finally setting oneself against the Spirit's work — not merely doubting, questioning, or even temporarily resisting, but permanently and knowingly opposing what one recognizes as the Spirit's activity. This is why it is unforgivable: not because God's grace is limited, but because the person has permanently closed the only door through which forgiveness can come.
responses.strengths: The emphasis on disposition rather than a single act relieves pastoral anxiety without trivializing the warning. The Pentecostal framework takes the Spirit's ongoing work seriously, making the sin a present-day possibility without making it a source of constant fear.
responses.weaknesses: The appeal to Greek tense can be overpressed — present tense does not always indicate ongoing action. Defining the sin as 'settled disposition' makes it nearly impossible to identify in any concrete case, which could weaken its force as a genuine warning.

responses.summary: Keener situates the saying in its Jewish rhetorical context. In Second Temple Judaism, blasphemy carried specific connotations of reviling God's name and work. The Pharisees' accusation was not ignorant — they were the theological elite, personally witnessing Jesus's exorcisms, and they attributed this manifest divine power to Beelzebul. Keener notes that in the ancient Mediterranean honor-shame culture, this was a deliberate public shaming of God's Spirit. The sin's unforgivability lies in its social and spiritual dynamics: it publicly defames the Spirit's work with full knowledge, poisoning the community's ability to recognize God's action. Keener argues this was a historically specific warning aimed at the religious leaders of Jesus's day, though the principle — that one can so persistently resist the Spirit's clear witness as to become unreachable — remains applicable.
responses.strengths: Rich historical and cultural contextualization. Explains why the Pharisees' particular act was uniquely culpable. Balances historical specificity with ongoing applicability.
responses.weaknesses: Heavy emphasis on socio-historical context may leave systematic theologians wanting a more precise doctrinal definition. The principle of 'persistent resistance' is broader than the specific act Jesus addresses.

responses.summary: The church fathers generally interpreted the blasphemy against the Spirit as final impenitence — the refusal to repent that persists until death. Augustine argued that this sin is not a single act but the comprehensive rejection of divine grace that endures to the end of life. Athanasius distinguished between speaking against Jesus in his humble human form (forgivable because of its ambiguity) and speaking against the Spirit's manifest power (unforgivable because it rejects unambiguous divine testimony). Chrysostom emphasized that the Pharisees had no excuse — they witnessed the miracles firsthand and could not claim ignorance. The patristic consensus held that this sin is not committed by those who sin and repent, nor by those who fall into doubt or fear, but only by those who permanently and knowingly set themselves against the Spirit. Augustine's formulation proved most influential: the sin against the Spirit is impenitence itself — the one sin that by definition excludes the possibility of forgiveness.
responses.strengths: Draws on centuries of careful theological reflection. Augustine's formulation elegantly explains why this sin alone is unforgivable — it is the refusal of forgiveness itself. Provides historical depth and pastoral comfort.
responses.weaknesses: The 'final impenitence' reading may stretch beyond what Jesus's words in their original context specifically meant. It risks transforming a concrete warning about a specific sin into a tautology — the unforgivable sin is unforgiveness.

responses.summary: Moo integrates the Synoptic passages with Hebrews 6:4-6 and 10:26-31, arguing that all three address the same fundamental reality from different angles: the possibility of so thoroughly rejecting the Spirit's witness that repentance becomes impossible — not because God withdraws the offer, but because the person has destroyed their own capacity to respond. In the Synoptics, this takes the form of attributing the Spirit to Satan; in Hebrews, it takes the form of apostasy after full enlightenment. Moo argues the common thread is not a specific act but a spiritual trajectory — a progressive hardening that moves from resistance to rejection to inability. This trajectory is visible only to God; no human observer can definitively identify it in another person. Pastorally, Moo emphasizes that the very existence of the warning functions as a means of grace: it shocks readers into self-examination and renewed dependence on the Spirit, which is precisely the opposite of the hardened disposition the warning describes.
responses.strengths: Synthesizes the relevant passages rather than treating them in isolation. The 'trajectory' model accounts for why this sin seems to be both a specific act and a general disposition. Strong pastoral application — the warning itself functions as a safeguard.
responses.weaknesses: The integration of Hebrews with the Synoptics assumes a unified theology across different authors, which some scholars question. The 'trajectory' model makes the sin essentially unidentifiable, which may weaken its function as a genuine warning.

context: Jesus warns that 'blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven' (Matt 12:31-32; Mark 3:28-30), spoken after the Pharisees attributed his Spirit-empowered exorcisms to Beelzebul. This has generated centuries of pastoral anxiety — countless Christians have feared they may have committed this sin. The parallel in Hebrews 6:4-6 raises related questions about apostasy and the limits of repentance.

key_verses.text: And so I tell you, every kind of sin and slander can be forgiven, but blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven. Anyone who speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but anyone who speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, either in this age or in the age to come.

Truly I tell you, people can be forgiven all their sins and every slander they utter, but whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will never be forgiven; they are guilty of an eternal sin. He said this because they were saying, 'He has an impure spirit.'

It is impossible for those who have once been enlightened, who have tasted the heavenly gift, who have shared in the Holy Spirit, who have tasted the goodness of the word of God and the powers of the coming age and who have fallen away, to be brought back to repentance.

consensus: Nearly all traditions agree that those who genuinely fear they have committed this sin almost certainly have not — the very concern demonstrates a responsive conscience incompatible with the settled, defiant rejection the passages describe. The historical context (Pharisees willfully attributing Christ's work to Satan) is widely recognized as the paradigmatic instance.

further_reading.title: Matthew (Expositor's Bible Commentary)

further_reading.author: Craig S. Keener
further_reading.title: The Gospel of Matthew: A Socio-Rhetorical Commentary

further_reading.author: Gareth Lee Cockerill
further_reading.title: The Epistle to the Hebrews (NICNT)

images.caption: Wrestling with hard texts — the darkness at the crucifixion

title: Can Believers Lose Salvation?
question: Does Hebrews teach that genuine Christians can fall away and be lost forever?

responses.summary: Schreiner argues that the descriptions in 6:4-5 — enlightenment, tasting the heavenly gift, sharing in the Spirit — can describe genuine spiritual experiences that fall short of saving regeneration. The Israelites in the wilderness experienced God's miraculous power, tasted manna from heaven, and shared in the Spirit's presence — yet most were not saved (1 Cor 10:1-5; Heb 3:7-19). Schreiner notes that the author himself distinguishes these people from genuine believers in 6:9: 'we are convinced of better things in your case — the things that have to do with salvation.' The warning is real — these people experienced genuine spiritual realities and are in genuine danger — but what they lacked was saving faith that perseveres. The function of the warning is to provoke genuine believers to examine themselves and press forward, while exposing those who were never truly regenerate.
responses.strengths: Takes the warning seriously without abandoning perseverance of the saints. The wilderness generation parallel is textually grounded — Hebrews 3 explicitly draws it. The distinction in 6:9 provides internal evidence for the reading.
responses.weaknesses: The cumulative weight of five participial descriptions ('enlightened,' 'tasted,' 'shared,' 'tasted,' 'tasted') makes the 'not really saved' reading strained — these are remarkably strong terms for mere external experience. Critics argue this empties the warning of its force for genuine believers.

responses.summary: Karen Jobes reads the warning as addressing genuine believers facing genuine danger — not as a hypothetical scenario but as a real possibility the author urgently wants to prevent. The descriptors in 6:4-5 are too strong and too numerous to describe mere external participation; they describe people who have genuinely experienced the new covenant realities of the Spirit. Jobes argues the warning functions prospectively — it describes what would happen if believers abandon Christ, precisely so that they will not. The author's confidence in 6:9 is not that apostasy is impossible but that his readers will heed the warning. This reading takes both the warning and the assurance seriously: the warning is a genuine means God uses to keep genuine believers persevering. Jobes notes that the original audience — Jewish Christians considering return to Judaism under social pressure — would have heard this not as an abstract theological puzzle but as a life-or-death pastoral appeal.
responses.strengths: Takes the full force of both the warning language and the assurance in 6:9. Attends to the pastoral context — real people under real pressure. The prospective reading preserves the warning's urgency without requiring that believers actually fall away.
responses.weaknesses: The prospective/preventative reading can become functionally identical to the hypothetical view — if the warning always prevents what it warns against, is the danger genuinely real? Some argue this domesticates the text.

responses.summary: Keener argues that the most natural reading of the Greek text is that genuinely converted people can genuinely apostatize. The five participial phrases in 6:4-5 describe authentic Christian experience — the language closely parallels how Hebrews describes believers elsewhere. The 'impossibility' of renewal is not about God's power but about the apostate's condition: having fully experienced and then fully rejected Christ, they have nowhere else to turn. Keener notes that the broader NT contains numerous warnings to believers about falling away (Gal 5:4; 2 Pet 2:20-22; Rev 2-3), and that the Reformed tradition's reinterpretation of these texts as addressing non-believers systematically neutralizes a category of biblical teaching. He argues the Arminian-Wesleyan reading preserves the genuine tension the NT maintains between assurance and warning — a tension that motivates both confidence and vigilance.
responses.strengths: Reads the text at face value without requiring the strong participial descriptions to mean less than they appear to. Accounts for the broader NT pattern of warnings to genuine believers. Preserves the pastoral urgency of the passage.
responses.weaknesses: Creates tension with passages that affirm perseverance (John 10:28-29; Rom 8:38-39; Phil 1:6). If genuine believers can fall away, the basis for Christian assurance becomes uncertain. Must explain why the author expresses confidence in 6:9 if the danger is so real.

responses.summary: Moo charts a middle course: the warning describes a real danger for people in a genuine relationship with God, and the warning itself is one of the means God uses to ensure that his people persevere. This is not a logical trick but a robust biblical pattern — God accomplishes his sovereign purposes through secondary means, including warnings, exhortations, and the community's pastoral care. The descriptions in 6:4-5 are genuine — these people have truly experienced the Spirit — and the danger is genuine — apostasy would be irrevocable. But the warning functions precisely to prevent the outcome it describes. Moo argues that 6:9 is the interpretive key: the author has just given the most severe warning imaginable, and then immediately says, 'We are convinced of better things in your case.' This is not contradiction but pastoral strategy — maximum warning followed by maximum encouragement.
responses.strengths: Preserves the genuine force of the warning without abandoning perseverance. The 'means of preservation' framework is theologically coherent — God uses warnings to keep people faithful. The reading of 6:9 as pastoral strategy is compelling.
responses.weaknesses: The 'warning as means' reading can seem like having it both ways — the danger is real, but it never actually happens to genuine believers. Critics from both Reformed and Arminian camps find this unsatisfying as a final resolution.

responses.summary: Calvin argued that the passage describes what would be the case if genuine believers fell away — but the very severity of the consequence demonstrates that God will not allow it to happen. The 'impossibility' of restoration reveals God's purpose in preserving his people: since apostasy would be irrecoverable (crucifying Christ afresh), God ensures that those truly united to Christ never reach that point. Calvin distinguished between temporary believers who experience spiritual gifts externally and the elect who receive the 'special inner call' that permanently transforms. He was less concerned with parsing the participial phrases than with affirming the theological conclusion: God's election is irrevocable (Rom 11:29), and those whom God genuinely saves are genuinely kept. The warning is genuine in its severity — it shows what is at stake — but its function is to terrify the complacent into renewed faith, not to describe the actual fate of any elect person.
responses.strengths: Consistent with Calvin's broader soteriology and the Reformed confessional tradition. Takes the severity of the warning seriously as a spur to perseverance. The distinction between 'temporary' and 'genuine' faith has precedent in the parable of the sower (Mark 4:16-17).
responses.weaknesses: The hypothetical reading ('if they could fall away, which they can't') risks making the warning toothless. Critics argue Calvin imports his systematic theology rather than reading Hebrews on its own terms. The participial descriptions seem to describe actual, not hypothetical, experience.

context: Hebrews 6:4-6 describes people who have been 'enlightened,' 'tasted the heavenly gift,' 'shared in the Holy Spirit,' and 'tasted the goodness of the word of God' — and then 'fallen away.' The author declares it 'impossible' to bring them back to repentance. This passage is the most debated text in the perseverance/apostasy debate, with the central question being whether those described were genuinely saved.

key_verses.text: It is impossible for those who have once been enlightened, who have tasted the heavenly gift, who have shared in the Holy Spirit, who have tasted the goodness of the word of God and the powers of the coming age and who have fallen away, to be brought back to repentance. To their loss they are crucifying the Son of God all over again and subjecting him to public disgrace.
Even though we speak like this, dear friends, we are convinced of better things in your case — the things that have to do with salvation.

I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all; no one can snatch them out of my Father's hand.

If we deliberately keep on sinning after we have received the knowledge of the truth, no sacrifice for sins is left, but only a fearful expectation of judgment and of raging fire that will consume the enemies of God.

consensus: Scholars universally acknowledge the passage describes genuine spiritual experience. The primary debate is whether these experiences necessarily constitute regeneration. Most evangelical interpreters affirm perseverance of genuine believers while taking the warning's force seriously. Arminian and Wesleyan scholars read the passage as a genuine warning that salvation can be forfeited.

further_reading.title: The Epistle to the Hebrews (NICNT)

further_reading.author: Thomas Schreiner and Ardel Caneday
further_reading.title: The Race Set Before Us: A Biblical Theology of Perseverance and Assurance

further_reading.author: Ben Witherington III
further_reading.title: Letters and Homilies for Jewish Christians: A Socio-Rhetorical Commentary on Hebrews, James and Jude

title: The Problem of Suffering
question: If God is all-powerful and all-good, why does He allow innocent suffering?

responses.summary: Goldingay argues that the book of Job deliberately deconstructs every neat theodicy — the friends' retribution theology, Elihu's disciplinary model, even Job's demand for a legal hearing — and replaces them all with encounter. When God finally speaks (Job 38-41), he offers no explanation for Job's suffering. Instead, he overwhelms Job with the vastness, complexity, and wildness of creation — a creation that includes Behemoth and Leviathan, forces of chaos that God controls but does not eliminate. Job's response is not intellectual satisfaction but worship: 'My ears had heard of you but now my eyes have seen you' (42:5). Goldingay contends that the book's genius is its refusal to answer the question on its own terms. The question 'Why do the innocent suffer?' presupposes a world in which God owes us explanations. Job's encounter reveals that God is not a moral accountant but a sovereign Creator whose ways exceed human comprehension. The answer to suffering is not a theory but a Person.
responses.strengths: Reads Job on its own literary and theological terms rather than extracting propositions from it. Honest about the limits of human understanding. The relational resolution honors the experience of sufferers who find comfort in God's presence, not in explanations.
responses.weaknesses: Critics argue that 'encounter instead of explanation' can function as an evasion — sufferers deserve more than 'trust me.' The relational answer may not satisfy those facing extreme, prolonged, or seemingly pointless suffering.

responses.summary: Longman argues that while Job offers no systematic theodicy, the broader canon provides a framework: suffering can be — though is not always — a means through which God accomplishes redemptive purposes. Romans 8:28-29 does not say suffering is good but that God works through all things, including suffering, for the ultimate good of conforming believers to Christ's image. The cross is the supreme example: the worst evil in human history became the means of salvation for the world. Longman is careful to distinguish this from the friends' retribution theology (which God rejects) and from glib pastoral platitudes. Not all suffering has a discernible purpose in this life, and claiming otherwise adds cruelty to pain. But the Christian hope is that God is not absent from suffering and that the final chapter has not been written — a hope grounded in the resurrection, which demonstrates God's power to bring life from death.
responses.strengths: Canonical breadth — integrates Job with Paul, Peter, and the cross. Avoids both glib answers and nihilistic despair. The cross as paradigm is theologically powerful — God enters suffering rather than explaining it away.
responses.weaknesses: The 'redemptive suffering' framework can be misused to glorify suffering or to tell sufferers their pain has a purpose they should appreciate. Does not adequately address suffering that appears to serve no redemptive purpose at all.

responses.summary: Habel reads Job as a profound act of theological protest. Job refuses to accept the friends' explanations, refuses to curse God as his wife suggests, and instead demands an audience with God — insisting on his innocence and God's accountability. Habel argues that God's speech from the whirlwind does not silence Job's protest but redirects it: the world is more complex than any human moral framework can contain. Crucially, God vindicates Job ('my servant Job has spoken the truth about me,' 42:7) and condemns the friends' tidy theology. Habel contends that the Bible legitimizes protest against suffering — the Psalms of lament, Habakkuk's complaints, and Job's lawsuit against God are all canonical. Faith is not passive acceptance but active engagement with a God who can handle our rage, grief, and accusation. The book's resolution is not an answer but a relationship restored through honest struggle.
responses.strengths: Takes the lament tradition seriously as a genuine theological mode, not a failure of faith. Honors Job's refusal to accept false comfort. The vindication of Job's 'honest speech' over the friends' pious platitudes is textually grounded and pastorally powerful.
responses.weaknesses: Can overemphasize the protest at the expense of Job's final submission (42:6). Some argue Habel's reading is shaped more by modern sensibilities about divine accountability than by the text's ancient context, where questioning God's justice is a far more radical act than modern readers appreciate.

responses.summary: Clines approaches Job as literature and argues that the book's power lies in its refusal to resolve the tension it creates. The prose framework (chs. 1-2, 42:7-17) offers one theology — patient Job is tested and restored. The poetic dialogue (chs. 3-41) offers another — angry Job challenges God and receives an overwhelming but non-explanatory divine response. Clines contends that these two theologies coexist in deliberate, unresolved tension. The book does not answer the problem of evil; it dramatizes the impossibility of answering it within the frameworks available to its characters. Clines pushes further: the divine speeches do not actually address Job's suffering at all — they redirect attention to the natural world's beauty and terror, implying that Job's moral framework is too small, not that it is wrong. The book leaves readers with a God who is present, powerful, and unfathomable — but not a God who explains.
responses.strengths: Rigorous literary reading that honors the book's complexity. Avoids forcing a resolution the text itself does not provide. The observation about the divine speeches' content — creation, not suffering — is textually acute.
responses.weaknesses: The purely literary approach may strip the text of its theological authority for believing communities. If Job offers no answer at all, its canonical function as wisdom literature is diminished. The emphasis on irresolution may reflect modern literary theory more than ancient wisdom conventions.

responses.summary: Brueggemann reads the problem of evil through the lens of Israel's lament traditions and the eschatological horizon of the canon. He argues that the Bible does not offer a philosophical theodicy but a narrative one: the story moves from creation through fall, exile, and suffering toward a promised restoration in which God will finally and completely defeat evil, wipe away tears, and make all things new (Rev 21:4). In the meantime, the faithful are invited into lament — the honest, anguished cry to God that refuses both passive resignation and atheistic despair. Lament is itself a form of faith: it addresses God as the one responsible and capable of action. Brueggemann contends that the modern church has largely lost the practice of lament, replacing it with triumphalism that cannot sustain people through genuine suffering. The biblical witness insists that the present is marked by real evil and real pain, and that faith means holding this reality together with the eschatological promise that God will prevail.
responses.strengths: Recovers the neglected biblical practice of lament as a legitimate theological and pastoral response. The eschatological framework provides genuine hope without minimizing present suffering. Avoids both glib answers and despair.
responses.weaknesses: The eschatological answer — 'God will fix it later' — may not satisfy those suffering now. The emphasis on lament, while valuable, does not directly address the philosophical argument. Some critics find Brueggemann's theology insufficiently robust on God's sovereignty.

context: The 'problem of evil' — if God is omnipotent and omnibenevolent, why does suffering exist? — is both a philosophical argument against theism and a deeply personal existential crisis. Scripture does not present a single systematic answer but engages the question through narrative (Job), lament (Psalms, Habakkuk), and theological argument (Romans 8). The book of Job is the Bible's most sustained treatment: Job's friends offer tidy theodicies that God himself ultimately rejects (Job 42:7-8).

key_verses.text: Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will depart. The LORD gave and the LORD has taken away; may the name of the LORD be praised.
After the LORD had said these things to Job, he said to Eliphaz the Temanite, 'I am angry with you and your two friends, because you have not spoken the truth about me, as my servant Job has.'
And we know that in all things God works for the good of those who love him, who have been called according to his purpose.
Your eyes are too pure to look on evil; you cannot tolerate wrongdoing. Why then do you tolerate the treacherous? Why are you silent while the wicked swallow up those more righteous than themselves?
He will wipe every tear from their eyes. There will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain, for the old order of things has passed away.

consensus: Scripture does not offer a single systematic theodicy. Job's resolution is relational rather than intellectual — encounter with God rather than explanation from God. Most Christian traditions affirm that suffering is real, God is good, evil will ultimately be defeated, and complete understanding is beyond human capacity in the present age.

further_reading.author: D.A. Carson
further_reading.title: How Long, O Lord? Reflections on Suffering and Evil

further_reading.author: Norman Habel
further_reading.title: The Book of Job: A Commentary (OTL)

further_reading.author: Alvin Plantinga
further_reading.title: God, Freedom, and Evil

title: Predestination and Human Choice
question: How can God predestine some for salvation while desiring all to be saved?

responses.summary: Schreiner argues that Romans 9 teaches unconditional election — God's choice of individuals for salvation based on his sovereign purpose, not foreseen faith or merit. Paul's argument moves deliberately: God chose Jacob over Esau before birth, before either had done anything good or bad (9:11), 'so that God's purpose in election might stand: not by works but by him who calls' (9:11-12). Schreiner contends that Paul anticipates the objection of unfairness (9:14) and responds not by softening the doctrine but by asserting God's sovereign freedom: 'I will have mercy on whom I have mercy' (9:15). The 2 Peter 3:9 and 1 Timothy 2:4 texts, Schreiner argues, use 'all' in context to mean 'all kinds of people' or 'all of the elect,' not every individual without exception. God genuinely desires the salvation of all whom he has chosen, and his sovereign purpose ensures they will come.
responses.strengths: Takes Romans 9 at face value in its immediate context. Paul's argument structure — objection, response, further elaboration — is best explained by unconditional election. Exegetically strong on the Romans 9 passage specifically.
responses.weaknesses: Must reinterpret the 'all' passages (1 Tim 2:4, 2 Pet 3:9) in non-obvious ways. Creates theological tension with God's expressed love for the world (John 3:16) and the universal offer of the gospel. Can appear to make God the author of reprobation.

responses.summary: Keener argues that Paul's primary concern in Romans 9 is not individual soteriological predestination but God's sovereign right to redefine the people of God — to include Gentiles and exclude unbelieving Israel. Jacob and Esau represent nations (as in Malachi 1:2-3), not individuals in eternity. Paul is defending God's faithfulness (9:6: 'It is not as though God's word had failed') by showing that God has always worked through chosen lines — and his current work through Christ is consistent with that pattern. Individual election, for Keener, is 'in Christ' — God chose the church as a body, and individuals enter the elect community by faith. This reading takes 2 Peter 3:9 and 1 Timothy 2:4 at face value: God genuinely desires all individuals to be saved, and faith is a genuine human response enabled by prevenient grace.
responses.strengths: Reads Romans 9 within the broader argument of Romans 9-11, which concerns Israel and the Gentiles as groups. Accounts for the universal salvation passages without reinterpretation. The corporate reading has strong support from the OT background of the Jacob/Esau texts.
responses.weaknesses: Romans 9:15 ('I will have mercy on whom I have mercy') and 9:18 ('he hardens whom he wants') use singular language that seems to address individuals. The corporate reading may underemphasize the individual dimension of Paul's argument.

responses.summary: Calvin insisted that Scripture teaches both divine sovereignty and human responsibility without offering a philosophical resolution. God genuinely commands all people to repent and believe; God also sovereignly determines who will respond. For Calvin, this is not a contradiction but a mystery that transcends human logic. He vigorously opposed the Arminian move of grounding election in foreseen faith — this, he argued, empties Romans 9 of its force, since Paul's whole point is that election precedes and causes faith, not the reverse. Calvin was equally insistent that God is not unjust: the reprobate are condemned for their own sin, not for a mere decree. Double predestination is a 'horrible decree' (decretum horribile) that Calvin affirmed with trembling, not with glib satisfaction. He counseled believers to look to Christ — not to the hidden decree — for assurance of election.
responses.strengths: Historically foundational for the Reformed tradition and deeply influential across five centuries. Takes both sets of texts seriously without domesticating either. Calvin's pastoral counsel — look to Christ, not the decree — addresses the existential anxiety the doctrine creates.
